question_frequency.rationale: The Pair poses end-of-turn 'want to extend?' questions but rarely engages the user before making decisions. At the two key decision points (parser strategy, token representation) it explicitly bails — 'Let me just proceed' and 'I'll just call it: named tuple is the right move' — turning potential didactic moments into monologues.

question_quality.rationale: The choices surfaced (shunting-yard vs recursive descent; named tuple vs parallel lists) do hit real design tensions, but the Pair answers them itself instead of letting the user reason through them. The follow-up offers ('unary minus, exponentiation, variables, error messages') are menu-style scope questions, not crux questions about the problem.

scaffolding.rationale: There is some progression (parser → error handling → caret messages), but each 'choice' is presented and immediately resolved by the Pair, so there's no real difficulty gradient for the learner. Spoilers are baked in — the recommended answer is given in the same breath as the alternatives.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and the explanations (precedence falling out of the call graph, walrus operator, ParseError subclassing) are genuinely useful and actionable. But conversational flow is one-directional: the Pair lectures and the user mostly affirms; there's little real back-and-forth.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code correctly handles +, -, *, /, parens, precedence, and adds nice caret-style error messages. It lacks unary minus that the baseline supports, but the user explicitly declared that out of scope, and all 5 happy-path + 3 error tests pass.

friction.rationale: Two turns / 158s vs baseline's one turn / 135s — only modestly longer, and the extra turn delivered real value (position-tracked errors). Pedagogical payoff per added length is modest because the Pair short-circuited its own didactic prompts, but overall friction is comparable to baseline.
